name: Katerra
summary: Construction technology company aiming to vertically integrate design, manufacturing, and construction of buildings; filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2021 after years of heavy losses and mismanagement.
lesson: Vertically integrating an entire industry's supply chain requires far more capital discipline and operational expertise than software-style scaling allows.
source: Wall Street Journal, TechCrunch bankruptcy coverage 2021

name: Blu Homes
summary: Prefabricated/modular green home manufacturer that struggled to scale factory production profitably and filed for bankruptcy in 2020.
lesson: Prefab housing economics depend on factory utilization; underused manufacturing capacity destroys unit economics quickly.
source: Builder Magazine, GreenBiz coverage of Blu Homes bankruptcy

name: Zeta Communities
summary: Modular green building manufacturer based in California that ceased operations around 2015 after failing to reach profitable scale.
lesson: Modular construction manufacturers need sustained order volume to cover fixed factory costs, which is hard to guarantee in a cyclical industry.
source: San Francisco Business Times coverage of Zeta Communities closure

name: Full Stack Modular
summary: Modular high-rise construction company, successor to the Forest City modular tower program in Brooklyn, that wound down after cost overruns and quality issues on its flagship project.
lesson: Unproven modular manufacturing techniques applied to complex high-rise projects can produce cost overruns that erase the promised savings.
source: The Real Deal, New York YIMBY coverage of modular tower issues

name: Cover
summary: Accessory dwelling unit (ADU) prefab manufacturer in Los Angeles that shut down operations in 2023 after failing to reach sustainable scale.
lesson: Small-scale prefab housing startups often can't achieve manufacturing economies of scale fast enough before capital runs out.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Cover ADU shutdown

name: Villa Homes
summary: Backyard ADU homebuilding startup that raised significant venture funding but ceased operations in 2022 amid a slowing housing market and high construction costs.
lesson: Construction-heavy business models are highly exposed to interest-rate and material-cost cycles that can wipe out margins quickly.
source: TechCrunch, The Information coverage of Villa Homes shutdown

name: Veev
summary: Prefabricated home construction and technology company that raised massive funding but shut down most operations in 2024 after failing to find a sustainable path to profitability.
lesson: Even well-capitalized construction-tech companies can burn through hundreds of millions without proving a profitable per-unit model.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Veev shutdown 2024

name: Blokable
summary: Modular multifamily housing manufacturer that wound down operations around 2023-2024 after struggling to secure enough project volume to sustain its factory.
lesson: Modular housing manufacturers need long-term pipeline commitments from developers before committing to factory-scale capital investment.
source: GeekWire coverage of Blokable wind-down

name: BuildDirect
summary: Online marketplace for building materials (flooring, décor) that filed for creditor protection (CCAA) in Canada in 2019 after years of losses.
lesson: Commoditized building-materials e-commerce faces thin margins that heavy customer-acquisition spending can quickly turn unprofitable.
source: BetaKit, Vancouver Sun coverage of BuildDirect CCAA filing

name: Kespry
summary: Drone data analytics company serving construction, mining, and insurance industries that shut down in 2022, selling off its technology assets.
lesson: Hardware-plus-software drone startups face brutal margin compression as commodity drone hardware becomes cheap and analytics alone struggle to fund the business.
source: TechCrunch, Commercial UAV News coverage of Kespry shutdown

name: PrecisionHawk
summary: Drone data and analytics company serving construction, agriculture, and energy that restructured and sold off assets in 2022 after failing to reach profitability.
lesson: Drone-data platforms that try to serve too many verticals at once dilute product focus and struggle to build a defensible niche.
source: TechCrunch, Commercial UAV News coverage of PrecisionHawk restructuring

name: Plant Prefab
summary: California-based prefab home manufacturer that laid off most staff and halted production in 2023 amid financial struggles despite high-profile backers.
lesson: Marquee investors and press attention do not substitute for proven manufacturing unit economics in capital-intensive housing.
source: The Real Deal, Dwell coverage of Plant Prefab struggles

name: Blueprint Robotics
summary: Baltimore-based panelized home-manufacturing company using robotic assembly lines that ceased operations and closed its factory in 2023.
lesson: Automating construction manufacturing does not help if end-market housing demand and pricing can't cover the factory's fixed costs.
source: Baltimore Business Journal coverage of Blueprint Robotics closure

name: Uptake Technologies
summary: Industrial predictive-maintenance AI company, serving construction and heavy-equipment fleets among other industries, that scaled back drastically and effectively wound down by the mid-2020s.
lesson: Predictive-maintenance AI needs deep, defensible data partnerships with equipment makers; without them, differentiation erodes quickly.
source: Crain's Chicago Business, Forbes coverage of Uptake's decline

name: Mighty Buildings
summary: 3D-printed prefab home manufacturer that raised substantial venture funding but underwent repeated mass layoffs and scaled back operations sharply in 2023-2024.
lesson: Novel construction manufacturing technology (3D printing) still must clear the same unit-economics bar as traditional homebuilding to reach scale.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Mighty Buildings layoffs

name: Sidewalk Labs
summary: Alphabet's urban-innovation subsidiary that proposed a high-tech 'smart city' district in Toronto (Quayside); the project was cancelled in 2020 and the unit was folded into Google in 2021.
lesson: Ambitious smart-city construction projects require sustained public trust and regulatory alignment that can collapse under privacy and governance concerns.
source: New York Times, The Verge coverage of Sidewalk Labs Quayside cancellation

name: Modulous
summary: UK-based modular building design software and platform company that entered financial administration in 2024 after failing to secure further funding.
lesson: Construction-tech platforms tied to a slowing housing development pipeline are highly vulnerable to funding-market downturns.
source: Construction News (UK) coverage of Modulous administration

name: Airware
summary: Drone operating-system and analytics company serving construction, mining, and insurance that shut down in 2018 after pivoting away from hardware failed to find a sustainable business.
lesson: Pivoting from hardware to software mid-flight can strand a company without a clear competitive moat in either market.
source: TechCrunch, The Verge coverage of Airware shutdown

name: 3D Robotics
summary: Drone manufacturer that abandoned consumer hardware after massive losses in 2015-2016, pivoted to construction/mapping software (Site Scan), and eventually wound down that business as well.
lesson: Competing on drone hardware against low-cost incumbents is a losing battle; pivoting to software after burning through hardware capital rarely leaves enough runway to succeed.
source: TechCrunch, IEEE Spectrum coverage of 3D Robotics history

name: Disperse
summary: UK-based computer-vision construction progress-monitoring startup that ceased operations around 2023 after struggling to raise further funding.
lesson: Construction progress-monitoring software faces a crowded competitive field, making it hard for smaller players to reach scale before funding dries up.
source: UK tech press coverage of Disperse closure

name: Prescient (AASI)
summary: Structural framing systems company for modular and multifamily construction that underwent significant financial restructuring around 2023-2024 amid a slowdown in multifamily construction starts.
lesson: Construction-technology suppliers tied to a single end-market (multifamily) are highly exposed to that market's cyclical downturns.
source: Trade press coverage of Prescient/AASI restructuring

name: Skycatch
summary: Drone data-processing company for construction and mining sites that underwent repeated large layoffs and scaled down to near-inactivity by the early 2020s.
lesson: Drone-data startups need a durable, high-margin software layer since drone hardware and basic mapping quickly become commoditized.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Skycatch layoffs and struggles

name: Guardhat
summary: Connected safety-wearables (smart hardhat) company for construction and industrial sites that faced significant financial difficulty and scaled back operations in the early 2020s.
lesson: Wearable hardware for construction safety faces long enterprise sales cycles that can outlast a startup's runway.
source: Trade press coverage of Guardhat's financial struggles

name: Q-Bot
summary: UK robotics company building robots for under-floor insulation retrofits in construction that entered financial administration amid funding difficulties in the mid-2020s.
lesson: Construction robotics serving a niche retrofit market can struggle to reach volume before capital runs out, especially when government retrofit incentives are uncertain.
source: UK construction trade press coverage of Q-Bot administration

name: Kittyhawk.io
summary: Drone fleet-management and airspace-compliance software company, used across construction and other industrial sectors, that shut down in 2021 after running out of funding.
lesson: Enterprise drone-operations software needs enough differentiated value to avoid being commoditized by larger platform players.
source: Commercial UAV News coverage of Kittyhawk.io shutdown

name: Assembly OSM
summary: Detroit-based modular/offsite home manufacturer that ceased manufacturing operations around 2023 amid funding and demand challenges.
lesson: Offsite manufacturing plants for affordable housing require steady, guaranteed order pipelines to stay solvent.
source: Detroit business press coverage of Assembly OSM

name: Volumetric Building Companies (VBC)
summary: Philadelphia-based modular construction manufacturer that faced severe financial distress and closed manufacturing capacity around 2023.
lesson: Modular manufacturers that scale factory capacity ahead of confirmed demand risk being crushed by fixed costs when orders slow.
source: Philadelphia Business Journal coverage of Volumetric Building Companies distress

name: Procore Technologies
summary: Cloud-based construction management software platform that IPO'd on Nasdaq in 2021 and became the category-defining leader in construction project management software.
lesson: Focusing relentlessly on a single vertical's workflow for two decades can build a durable category leader.
source: Procore S-1 filing, Nasdaq IPO coverage 2021

name: PlanGrid
summary: Construction blueprint and field collaboration software acquired by Autodesk in 2018 for approximately $875 million.
lesson: Digitizing a painful paper-based workflow for a large, underserved industry can command a premium acquisition price.
source: Autodesk press release, TechCrunch coverage of PlanGrid acquisition 2018

name: BuildingConnected
summary: Construction bid management and preconstruction network acquired by Autodesk in 2018 to complement its construction cloud offering.
lesson: Building a two-sided network connecting general contractors and subcontractors for bidding creates defensible network effects attractive to larger platforms.
source: Autodesk press release on BuildingConnected acquisition 2018

name: Assemble Systems
summary: Cloud-based BIM data management software acquired by Autodesk in 2017 to strengthen its construction cloud data platform.
lesson: Solving a specific technical data-management pain point can lead to a strategic acquisition even with modest funding.
source: Autodesk press release on Assemble Systems acquisition 2017

name: Sefaira
summary: Building performance and sustainability analysis software acquired by Trimble in 2016.
lesson: Niche sustainability analytics tools can find a home inside larger AEC software suites through acquisition.
source: Trimble press release on Sefaira acquisition 2016

name: Fieldlens
summary: Real-time construction field communication and punch-list app acquired by Autodesk in 2015 and folded into BIM 360.
lesson: Simple, focused field-communication tools can become acquisition targets for platforms building out a broader construction cloud suite.
source: Autodesk press release on Fieldlens acquisition 2015

name: Aconex
funding: IPO on ASX 2014; acquired by Oracle 2018 for $1.19B
summary: Australian construction project collaboration and document management platform that went public on the ASX in 2014 and was acquired by Oracle in 2018 for approximately $1.19 billion.
lesson: Global construction collaboration platforms with strong document-control network effects can reach billion-dollar exits.
source: Oracle press release, ASX filings on Aconex acquisition 2018

name: Textura
funding: IPO on Nasdaq 2013; acquired by Oracle 2016 for $663M
summary: Construction payment management software that IPO'd in 2013 and was later acquired by Oracle in 2016 for approximately $663 million.
lesson: Payment and compliance management software addressing a specific pain point in construction subcontractor payments can sustain a public listing and later strategic acquisition.
source: Oracle press release on Textura acquisition 2016

name: Viewpoint Construction Software
summary: Construction ERP and project management software company acquired by Trimble in 2018 for approximately $1.2 billion.
lesson: Deep, long-tenured relationships with construction back-office workflows can command large strategic acquisition premiums.
source: Trimble press release on Viewpoint acquisition 2018

name: e-Builder
summary: Construction program management software acquired by Trimble in 2018 to expand its owner-side construction software portfolio.
lesson: Serving construction project owners, not just contractors, opens a distinct, valuable software niche.
source: Trimble press release on e-Builder acquisition 2018

name: Bluebeam
summary: PDF markup and collaboration software widely used in construction document review, acquired by Nemetschek Group in 2014.
lesson: A capital-efficient, product-led company can grow to a large exit without heavy venture funding.
source: Nemetschek press release on Bluebeam acquisition 2014

name: Fieldwire
summary: Construction field management and task-tracking app acquired by Hilti in 2021 to extend Hilti's digital tools ecosystem.
lesson: Construction field software focused on simplicity and mobile usability can attract strategic buyers outside pure software companies, like tool manufacturers.
source: Hilti press release on Fieldwire acquisition 2021

name: Levelset
summary: Construction payment and mechanics-lien management software (formerly zlien) acquired by Procore in 2021 for approximately $500 million.
lesson: Solving a specific legal/financial compliance pain point for contractors can build significant standalone value.
source: Procore press release on Levelset acquisition 2021

name: Join
summary: Preconstruction bid-leveling and collaboration software acquired by Autodesk in 2023 to strengthen its preconstruction offering.
lesson: Modernizing a specific, painful preconstruction workflow remains an attractive tuck-in acquisition target for platform incumbents.
source: Autodesk press release on Join acquisition 2023

name: GoContractor
summary: Construction worker safety onboarding and orientation software acquired by Highwire (formerly Newmetrix) in 2021 as part of a construction safety-tech consolidation.
lesson: Niche compliance software can find scale through consolidation with adjacent construction safety platforms.
source: Highwire press release on GoContractor acquisition 2021

name: Building Robotics (Comfy)
summary: Smart building/workplace comfort software (Comfy) acquired by Siemens in 2018 to strengthen its building technologies portfolio.
lesson: Employee-facing smart building software can be an attractive bolt-on for large industrial building-systems companies.
source: Siemens press release on Building Robotics/Comfy acquisition 2018

name: Bractlet
summary: Building energy analytics and digital-twin software company acquired by CBRE in 2022 to enhance CBRE's building performance offerings.
lesson: Building performance analytics can find a strategic home within large real-estate services firms even after a difficult fundraising environment.
source: CBRE press release on Bractlet acquisition 2022

name: Reconstruct
summary: Construction site reality-capture and progress analytics platform acquired by Trimble in 2021.
lesson: Reality-capture and progress-tracking technology is a natural extension for established construction software platforms to acquire rather than build.
source: Trimble press release on Reconstruct acquisition 2021

name: StructionSite
summary: 360-degree jobsite photo documentation and progress-tracking startup acquired by DroneDeploy in 2021.
lesson: Reality-capture startups focused on construction documentation can be acquired to broaden a drone/mapping platform's vertical reach.
source: DroneDeploy press release on StructionSite acquisition 2021

name: Smartvid.io
summary: AI-based construction safety and risk analytics platform acquired by Autodesk in 2021 and combined into Autodesk Construction Cloud's safety offering.
lesson: AI safety-analytics tools trained on construction site photo/video data become valuable inputs for larger construction cloud platforms.
source: Autodesk press release on Smartvid.io acquisition 2021

name: Indus.ai
summary: Computer-vision construction site monitoring and analytics company acquired by Procore in 2020, later rebranded as Procore AI.
lesson: Computer-vision jobsite monitoring is a natural feature acquisition for project management platforms seeking to add AI capabilities.
source: Procore press release on Indus.ai acquisition 2020

name: Pype
summary: AI-powered construction project closeout and compliance software acquired by Procore in 2020.
lesson: Automating tedious compliance and closeout paperwork is a valuable niche that platform players are willing to acquire rather than build in-house.
source: Procore press release on Pype acquisition 2020

name: Honest Buildings
summary: Capital project management software for real estate owners acquired by JLL in 2019 to expand JLL's project and development services technology.
lesson: Software serving real estate owners' capital project oversight needs can be a strategic fit for large real-estate services firms.
source: JLL press release on Honest Buildings acquisition 2019

name: Matterport
summary: 3D spatial data and digital-twin capture company widely used in construction/AEC documentation that went public via SPAC merger in 2021 and continues operating as a public company.
lesson: Building a broad spatial-data capture platform that serves multiple verticals can support a durable public company.
source: Matterport SEC filings, SPAC merger press coverage 2021

name: DroneDeploy
summary: Drone mapping and analytics software company serving construction, agriculture, and other industries that has grown into an industry-leading platform while remaining private.
lesson: A software-first, rather than hardware-first, drone-data strategy avoids the margin pressure that sank many drone hardware competitors.
source: Crunchbase, TechCrunch funding coverage of DroneDeploy

name: Buildertrend
funding: Majority stake sold to Insight Venture Partners 2018
summary: Construction project management software for homebuilders and remodelers that has grown into one of the largest privately held platforms in residential construction tech.
lesson: Focusing on an underserved segment, like residential builders and remodelers, rather than competing directly with commercial construction software leaders can build a large, durable business.
source: Buildertrend company press releases, Insight Partners investment coverage

name: CompanyCam
summary: Photo documentation and job-site communication app for contractors that has grown rapidly and remains an independent, thriving company.
lesson: A simple, focused tool solving one clear pain point for tradespeople can scale into a large customer base efficiently.
source: TechCrunch, company funding announcements for CompanyCam

name: Built Technologies
summary: Construction lending and payment/draw management software platform serving banks and contractors that has raised substantial funding and continues to grow.
lesson: Digitizing the construction loan draw process addresses a large, underserved fintech niche connecting lenders, owners, and contractors.
source: Built Technologies funding announcements, TechCrunch coverage

name: ICON
summary: 3D-printing construction technology company that has built homes, military housing, and partnered with NASA on lunar construction research, remaining a well-funded industry leader.
lesson: Combining novel construction technology with diversified customers reduces reliance on any single market.
source: ICON company press releases, TechCrunch funding coverage

name: Doxel
summary: AI-powered construction progress-tracking and analytics platform using computer vision that continues to operate and raise funding.
lesson: AI progress-tracking tools that directly tie to cost and schedule outcomes give construction owners a clear ROI case.
source: TechCrunch, Doxel funding announcements

name: OpenSpace
summary: 360-degree jobsite photo capture and AI documentation platform that has raised substantial funding and become widely adopted across large general contractors.
lesson: Automating jobsite photo documentation with minimal workflow disruption drives fast adoption across general contractors.
source: OpenSpace funding announcements, TechCrunch coverage

name: Buildots
summary: Israeli computer-vision construction progress-monitoring company that has raised significant funding and expanded internationally.
lesson: Combining hard-hat mounted cameras with AI progress analytics can create a differentiated, defensible construction monitoring product.
source: Buildots funding announcements, Calcalist/TechCrunch coverage

name: nPlan
summary: UK-based AI company that predicts construction project schedule risk using historical project data, continuing to operate and expand.
lesson: Applying machine learning to historical project data to forecast schedule risk gives construction owners a differentiated planning tool.
source: nPlan funding announcements, UK tech press coverage

name: Built Robotics
summary: Autonomous construction equipment retrofit company that continues to operate and expand its autonomy technology across heavy equipment fleets.
lesson: Retrofitting autonomy onto existing heavy equipment, rather than building new hardware from scratch, lowers capital intensity and speeds adoption.
source: Built Robotics funding announcements, TechCrunch coverage

name: Trunk Tools
summary: Generative AI platform for construction project data and documents that has raised substantial funding amid strong enterprise adoption.
lesson: Applying generative AI to unlock unstructured construction project documents addresses a widespread, expensive information-retrieval problem.
source: Trunk Tools funding announcements, TechCrunch coverage

name: Homebound
summary: Technology-enabled homebuilding platform, originally built to help wildfire survivors rebuild, that has continued to raise funding and expand its build-to-order homebuilding model.
lesson: Combining project-management software with an actual homebuilding services business can create a differentiated, technology-enabled builder.
source: Homebound funding announcements, TechCrunch coverage

name: PlanRadar
summary: Austria-based construction and real estate documentation/defect-management software that has expanded across dozens of countries while remaining independent.
lesson: Construction documentation software can scale internationally by focusing on straightforward defect and task tracking rather than complex ERP features.
source: PlanRadar funding announcements, European tech press coverage

name: Sablono
summary: Germany-based construction progress and workflow-tracking software that continues to operate and serve major infrastructure and building projects across Europe.
lesson: Detailed workflow and task-tracking software for complex construction projects can sustain a profitable niche in the European market.
source: Sablono company announcements, German tech press coverage

name: Kreo
summary: UK-based construction cost-estimating and takeoff software company using automation and cloud collaboration that continues to raise funding and grow.
lesson: Automating the traditionally manual, error-prone construction estimating process addresses a high-value pain point for contractors.
source: Kreo funding announcements, UK tech press coverage

name: COBOD International
funding: Backed by GE Renewable Energy, CEMEX, Holcim (undisclosed amounts)
summary: Danish 3D-printing construction technology company (BOD2 printer) that has built homes, schools, and infrastructure projects globally and partnered with major building materials companies.
lesson: Partnering directly with major cement and building-materials companies gives 3D-printing construction technology both capital and distribution advantages.
source: COBOD company press releases, construction trade press coverage

name: Fastbrick Robotics (FBR Ltd)
funding: Publicly listed on ASX; multiple capital raises per company filings
summary: Australian robotics company developing the Hadrian X autonomous bricklaying robot, listed on the Australian Securities Exchange and continuing commercial deployment.
lesson: Going public early allowed a capital-intensive construction robotics company to fund years of hardware R&D before reaching full commercial scale.
source: FBR Ltd ASX filings and investor announcements

name: CoConstruct
summary: Residential construction and remodeling project management software that merged with competitor Buildertrend in 2021 to form a combined market leader.
lesson: Two capital-efficient, complementary residential construction software companies merging can create more value than continuing to compete for the same niche.
source: Buildertrend/CoConstruct merger announcement 2021

name: HeadLight
summary: Construction inspection documentation and photo-verification software acquired by Bentley Systems in 2023 to expand its infrastructure asset management offerings.
lesson: Focused inspection-documentation tools for infrastructure owners can be attractive tuck-in acquisitions for large asset-management software platforms.
source: Bentley Systems press release on HeadLight acquisition 2023
